planets.name: Mercury
planets.answer: Mercury is the smallest planet and the closest planet to the Sun. It has almost no atmosphere, so days are extremely hot and nights are extremely cold.

planets.name: Venus
planets.answer: Venus is the hottest planet because its thick carbon dioxide atmosphere traps heat. It is similar in size to Earth, but its surface is far too hot and pressurized for humans.

planets.name: Earth
planets.answer: Earth is our home planet. It has liquid water, breathable air, land, oceans, and life, which makes it very special in our solar system.

planets.name: Mars
planets.answer: Mars is called the Red Planet because iron-rich dust gives its surface a rusty red color. Scientists study Mars to learn whether life ever existed there.

planets.name: Jupiter
planets.answer: Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system. It is a gas giant with many moons and a huge storm called the Great Red Spot.

planets.name: Saturn
planets.answer: Saturn is famous for its bright rings made of ice, rock, and dust. It is a gas giant and is much larger than Earth.

planets.name: Uranus
planets.answer: Uranus is an ice giant that rotates on its side. Its blue-green color comes from methane gas in its atmosphere.

planets.name: Neptune
planets.answer: Neptune is the farthest major planet from the Sun. It is an ice giant with powerful winds and a deep blue color.

planets.name: Dwarf Planets
planets.answer: Dwarf planets are worlds that orbit the Sun but have not cleared their orbital path. Pluto, Ceres, Eris, Haumea, and Makemake are examples.

planets.name: Solar System
planets.answer: The solar system includes the Sun, eight planets, dwarf planets, moons, asteroids, comets, dust, and gas. Gravity keeps all these objects moving around the Sun.

planets.name: Planet
planets.answer: A planet is a large round object that orbits a star and does not make its own light. Planets shine because they reflect light from their star.

planets.name: Terrestrial Planets
planets.answer: Terrestrial planets are rocky planets with solid surfaces. Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars are the rocky planets in our solar system.

planets.name: Gas Giants
planets.answer: Gas giants are huge planets made mostly of hydrogen and helium. Jupiter and Saturn are gas giants in our solar system.

planets.name: Ice Giants
planets.answer: Ice giants are large planets with gases plus icy materials like water, ammonia, and methane. Uranus and Neptune are ice giants.

planets.name: Planetary Atmosphere
planets.answer: A planetary atmosphere is a layer of gases around a planet. Atmospheres can protect planets, affect temperature, and create weather.

stars.name: Sun
stars.answer: The Sun is a huge star at the center of our solar system. It gives Earth the light and heat needed for life.

stars.name: Star
stars.answer: A star is a giant ball of hot gas that makes light and heat through nuclear fusion. The Sun is the closest star to Earth.

stars.name: Nuclear Fusion
stars.answer: Nuclear fusion is the process that powers stars. In a star's core, hydrogen atoms join to form helium and release a huge amount of energy.

stars.name: Red Giant
stars.answer: A red giant is an older star that has expanded and cooled on the outside. Our Sun will become a red giant far in the future.

stars.name: White Dwarf
stars.answer: A white dwarf is the hot leftover core of a small or medium star after it runs out of fuel. It slowly cools over a very long time.

stars.name: Supernova
stars.answer: A supernova is a giant star explosion. It can create heavy elements and leave behind a neutron star or black hole.

stars.name: Neutron Star
stars.answer: A neutron star is an extremely dense leftover core from a massive star. A sugar-cube-sized piece would have enormous mass.

stars.name: Binary Stars
stars.answer: Binary stars are two stars that orbit each other. Many stars in the galaxy are part of binary or multiple-star systems.

stars.name: Sirius
stars.answer: Sirius, also called the Dog Star, is the brightest star in Earth's night sky. It is actually a two-star system.

stars.name: Light Year
stars.answer: A light-year is a distance, not a time. It is how far light travels in one year, about 9.46 trillion kilometers.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Galaxy
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: A galaxy is a giant group of stars, gas, dust, planets, and dark matter held together by gravity. Our solar system is in the Milky Way galaxy.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Milky Way
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: The Milky Way is our home galaxy. It is a spiral galaxy with billions of stars, including our Sun.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Andromeda Galaxy
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: The Andromeda Galaxy is the nearest large spiral galaxy to the Milky Way. It is slowly moving toward our galaxy.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Spiral Galaxy
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: A spiral galaxy has a flat disk with curved arms of stars, gas, and dust. The Milky Way is a spiral galaxy.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Elliptical Galaxy
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: An elliptical galaxy looks round or oval and often contains many older stars. It usually has less gas and dust than a spiral galaxy.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Nebula
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: A nebula is a huge cloud of gas and dust in space. Some nebulae are star nurseries where new stars form.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Orion Nebula
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: The Orion Nebula is a bright star-forming cloud in the Orion constellation. It is one of the easiest nebulae to see with binoculars or a small telescope.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Crab Nebula
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: The Crab Nebula is the leftover cloud from a supernova seen from Earth in 1054. A fast-spinning neutron star sits near its center.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Planetary Nebula
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: A planetary nebula forms when a dying Sun-like star sheds its outer layers. It is not actually a planet; early telescopes just made it look planet-shaped.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Dark Matter
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: Dark matter is invisible material that does not give off light, but its gravity affects galaxies. Scientists know it exists because galaxies move as if extra mass is present.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Dark Energy
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: Dark energy is the name for the mysterious cause of the universe's faster expansion. Scientists are still trying to understand what it really is.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Big Bang
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: The Big Bang is the idea that the universe began in a very hot, dense state about 13.8 billion years ago and has been expanding ever since.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Universe
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: The universe is everything that exists: space, time, matter, energy, planets, stars, galaxies, and more.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Cosmic Microwave Background
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: The cosmic microwave background is faint leftover radiation from the early universe. It helps scientists study what the universe was like when it was young.

galaxies_and_cosmos.name: Gravity
galaxies_and_cosmos.answer: Gravity is the force that pulls objects with mass toward each other. It keeps planets in orbit, holds galaxies together, and pulls us toward Earth.

constellations.name: Constellations
constellations.answer: Constellations are official patterns or regions of stars in the sky. Astronomers use 88 constellations like a map for finding objects in space.

constellations.name: Orion
constellations.answer: Orion is a famous constellation that looks like a hunter. Its three bright belt stars are easy to spot in the night sky.

constellations.name: Ursa Major
constellations.answer: Ursa Major means Great Bear. It contains the Big Dipper pattern, which can help people find the North Star.

constellations.name: Ursa Minor
constellations.answer: Ursa Minor means Little Bear. Polaris, the North Star, is part of this constellation and helps show the direction north.

constellations.name: Cassiopeia
constellations.answer: Cassiopeia is a constellation that often looks like a W or M in the sky. It is useful for finding your way around the northern sky.

constellations.name: Scorpius
constellations.answer: Scorpius is a constellation shaped like a scorpion. It contains Antares, a bright reddish star.

constellations.name: Leo
constellations.answer: Leo is a constellation named after a lion. It is one of the zodiac constellations and is easy to recognize by its sickle-shaped pattern.

constellations.name: Zodiac
constellations.answer: The zodiac is a band of constellations along the Sun's apparent path in the sky. Astronomers use it to describe positions, but astrology is not science.

small_bodies.name: Asteroid
small_bodies.answer: Asteroids are rocky objects that orbit the Sun. Many are found in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter.

small_bodies.name: Asteroid Belt
small_bodies.answer: The asteroid belt is a region between Mars and Jupiter where many rocky asteroids orbit the Sun.

small_bodies.name: Comet
small_bodies.answer: Comets are icy objects that orbit the Sun. When they get close to the Sun, ice turns to gas and can form a glowing tail.

small_bodies.name: Kuiper Belt
small_bodies.answer: The Kuiper Belt is a faraway region beyond Neptune filled with icy objects. Pluto is one of its most famous worlds.

small_bodies.name: Oort Cloud
small_bodies.answer: The Oort Cloud is a distant, icy cloud thought to surround the solar system. Many long-period comets may come from there.

small_bodies.name: Meteor
small_bodies.answer: A meteor is the bright streak of light you see when a small space rock burns up in Earth's atmosphere. People often call it a shooting star.

small_bodies.name: Meteoroid
small_bodies.answer: A meteoroid is a small rock or metal object traveling through space. If it enters Earth's atmosphere and glows, it becomes a meteor.

small_bodies.name: Meteorite
small_bodies.answer: A meteorite is a piece of space rock that survives its fall through the atmosphere and lands on Earth's surface.

small_bodies.name: Meteor Shower
small_bodies.answer: A meteor shower happens when Earth passes through dust left by a comet or asteroid. Many meteors can appear in the sky during the shower.

small_bodies.name: Near-Earth Object
small_bodies.answer: A near-Earth object is an asteroid or comet whose orbit brings it close to Earth. Scientists track them carefully for safety.

spaceflight.name: Rocket
spaceflight.answer: A rocket is a vehicle that pushes itself forward by throwing hot gas backward. Rockets can work in space because they carry their own fuel and oxygen source.

spaceflight.name: Rocket Launch
spaceflight.answer: A rocket launch is when a rocket lifts off from Earth and carries people, satellites, cargo, or science tools into space.

spaceflight.name: Orbit
spaceflight.answer: An orbit is the curved path one object follows around another because of gravity. Earth orbits the Sun, and the Moon orbits Earth.

spaceflight.name: Satellite
spaceflight.answer: A satellite is an object that orbits a planet or moon. Some are natural, like the Moon, and some are human-made for communication, weather, navigation, and science.

spaceflight.name: Space Station
spaceflight.answer: A space station is a large spacecraft where astronauts can live and work while orbiting Earth. It is like a laboratory in space.

spaceflight.name: International Space Station
spaceflight.answer: The ISS, or International Space Station, is a science laboratory that orbits Earth. Astronauts live and work there while studying space, medicine, materials, and Earth.

spaceflight.name: Tiangong
spaceflight.answer: Tiangong is China's space station in low Earth orbit. Astronauts live there to perform science experiments and test space technology.

spaceflight.name: Astronaut
spaceflight.answer: An astronaut is a person trained to travel and work in space. Different countries may also use words like cosmonaut or vyomanaut.

spaceflight.name: Spacesuit
spaceflight.answer: A spacesuit protects astronauts from vacuum, extreme temperatures, and harmful radiation. It also provides oxygen and helps remove carbon dioxide.

spaceflight.name: Reentry
spaceflight.answer: Reentry is when a spacecraft comes back into Earth's atmosphere. Heat shields protect it from the intense heat caused by fast-moving air.

spaceflight.name: Reusable Rocket
spaceflight.answer: A reusable rocket can fly more than once. Landing and reusing boosters can reduce the cost of launching things into space.

spaceflight.name: Payload
spaceflight.answer: A payload is the cargo carried by a rocket, such as a satellite, spacecraft, telescope, or supplies for astronauts.

spaceflight.name: Launch Pad
spaceflight.answer: A launch pad is the place where a rocket stands before liftoff. It includes support towers, fuel systems, safety equipment, and control systems.

spaceflight.name: Low Earth Orbit
spaceflight.answer: Low Earth orbit is a region fairly close to Earth where many satellites and the ISS travel. It is commonly used for science, Earth observation, and communication.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Apollo
missions_and_telescopes.answer: Apollo was NASA's program that sent astronauts to the Moon. Apollo 11 landed humans on the Moon in 1969.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Artemis
missions_and_telescopes.answer: Artemis is NASA's program to return astronauts to the Moon and prepare for future missions to Mars.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Voyager
missions_and_telescopes.answer: Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 are NASA spacecraft launched in 1977. They explored outer planets and now travel through interstellar space or toward it.

missions_and_telescopes.name: James Webb Space Telescope
missions_and_telescopes.answer: The James Webb Space Telescope observes the universe mostly in infrared light. It studies early galaxies, star formation, exoplanets, and distant objects.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Hubble Space Telescope
missions_and_telescopes.answer: The Hubble Space Telescope has taken famous images of planets, nebulae, galaxies, and deep space. It observes mostly visible and ultraviolet light.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Chandrayaan
missions_and_telescopes.answer: Chandrayaan is India's Moon mission series by ISRO. These missions study the Moon's surface, minerals, and polar regions.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Mangalyaan
missions_and_telescopes.answer: Mangalyaan, also called the Mars Orbiter Mission, was India's first mission to Mars. It showed ISRO could reach Mars orbit with smart engineering.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Gaganyaan
missions_and_telescopes.answer: Gaganyaan is ISRO's human spaceflight program. Its goal is to send Indian astronauts into space and bring them safely back to Earth.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Aditya-L1
missions_and_telescopes.answer: Aditya-L1 is India's solar mission to study the Sun. It observes solar activity, the solar wind, and space weather from a stable point in space.

missions_and_telescopes.name: PSLV
missions_and_telescopes.answer: PSLV stands for Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle. It is one of ISRO's trusted rockets for launching satellites into different orbits.

missions_and_telescopes.name: GSLV
missions_and_telescopes.answer: GSLV stands for Geosynchronous Satellite Launch Vehicle. ISRO uses it to launch heavier satellites, including communication satellites.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Falcon 9
missions_and_telescopes.answer: Falcon 9 is a SpaceX rocket with a reusable first stage. It launches satellites, cargo, and crew missions.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Starship
missions_and_telescopes.answer: Starship is SpaceX's large fully reusable rocket system under development. It is designed for carrying cargo and people to orbit, the Moon, and possibly Mars.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Blue Origin New Shepard
missions_and_telescopes.answer: New Shepard is Blue Origin's reusable suborbital rocket. It is designed to carry experiments and people briefly above the edge of space.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Parker Solar Probe
missions_and_telescopes.answer: Parker Solar Probe is a NASA spacecraft that flies very close to the Sun. It studies the solar corona and solar wind.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Mars Rovers
missions_and_telescopes.answer: Mars rovers are robotic vehicles that drive on Mars. They study rocks, soil, weather, and clues about ancient water.

missions_and_telescopes.name: Lunar Rover
missions_and_telescopes.answer: A lunar rover is a vehicle made to move on the Moon. Rovers can carry instruments, cameras, and sometimes astronauts.

agencies_and_companies.name: NASA
agencies_and_companies.answer: NASA is the space agency of the United States. It studies Earth, planets, stars, galaxies, and sends spacecraft and astronauts into space.

agencies_and_companies.name: ISRO
agencies_and_companies.answer: ISRO is India's space agency. It builds satellites, launches rockets, and runs missions like Chandrayaan, Mangalyaan, Gaganyaan, and Aditya-L1.

agencies_and_companies.name: ESA
agencies_and_companies.answer: ESA is the European Space Agency. It works on rockets, satellites, Earth observation, science missions, and international space projects.

agencies_and_companies.name: JAXA
agencies_and_companies.answer: JAXA is Japan's space agency. It works on satellites, asteroid missions, rockets, science spacecraft, and astronaut activities.

agencies_and_companies.name: Roscosmos
agencies_and_companies.answer: Roscosmos is Russia's space agency. It has worked on human spaceflight, rockets, satellites, and space station missions.

agencies_and_companies.name: SpaceX
agencies_and_companies.answer: SpaceX is a private space company known for Falcon 9, Dragon spacecraft, Starlink satellites, and the Starship rocket system.

agencies_and_companies.name: Blue Origin
agencies_and_companies.answer: Blue Origin is a private space company that builds reusable rockets and space technology. Its vehicles include New Shepard and New Glenn.

agencies_and_companies.name: Commercial Spaceflight
agencies_and_companies.answer: Commercial spaceflight means private companies build or operate space vehicles and services. They can launch satellites, cargo, astronauts, and experiments.

black_holes_and_extreme_space.name: Black Hole
black_holes_and_extreme_space.answer: A black hole is a region in space where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape after crossing the event horizon.

black_holes_and_extreme_space.name: Event Horizon
black_holes_and_extreme_space.answer: The event horizon is the boundary around a black hole. Once something crosses it, it cannot escape the black hole's gravity.

black_holes_and_extreme_space.name: Singularity
black_holes_and_extreme_space.answer: A singularity is the extremely dense center predicted inside a black hole. Scientists use advanced physics to study what might happen there.

black_holes_and_extreme_space.name: Supermassive Black Hole
black_holes_and_extreme_space.answer: A supermassive black hole is a black hole with millions or billions of times the Sun's mass. Many large galaxies have one at their center.

black_holes_and_extreme_space.name: Accretion Disk
black_holes_and_extreme_space.answer: An accretion disk is a spinning disk of hot gas and dust falling toward a compact object like a black hole. It can glow very brightly.

black_holes_and_extreme_space.name: Quasar
black_holes_and_extreme_space.answer: A quasar is an extremely bright center of a distant galaxy powered by a supermassive black hole feeding on gas and dust.

earth_and_observation.name: Moon
earth_and_observation.answer: The Moon is Earth's natural satellite. It reflects sunlight, affects ocean tides, and is the only world beyond Earth where humans have walked.

earth_and_observation.name: Moon Phases
earth_and_observation.answer: Moon phases happen because we see different sunlit parts of the Moon as it orbits Earth. Main phases include new moon, first quarter, full moon, and last quarter.

earth_and_observation.name: Eclipse
earth_and_observation.answer: An eclipse happens when one space object blocks light from another. A solar eclipse involves the Moon blocking the Sun, and a lunar eclipse involves Earth shading the Moon.

earth_and_observation.name: Tides
earth_and_observation.answer: Tides are the rise and fall of ocean water mainly caused by the Moon's gravity and also affected by the Sun's gravity.

earth_and_observation.name: Aurora
earth_and_observation.answer: Auroras are colorful lights near Earth's poles. They happen when charged particles from the Sun interact with gases in Earth's atmosphere.

earth_and_observation.name: Space Weather
earth_and_observation.answer: Space weather is activity from the Sun that can affect Earth and satellites. Strong solar storms can disturb radio signals, power systems, and auroras.

earth_and_observation.name: Telescope
earth_and_observation.answer: A telescope gathers light to help us see faraway objects. Some telescopes use lenses, some use mirrors, and some work in space.

earth_and_observation.name: Radio Telescope
earth_and_observation.answer: A radio telescope detects radio waves from space. It can study objects that may be invisible to normal optical telescopes.

earth_and_observation.name: Spectroscopy
earth_and_observation.answer: Spectroscopy splits light into colors or wavelengths. Scientists use it to learn what stars, planets, and galaxies are made of.

earth_and_observation.name: NASA APOD
earth_and_observation.answer: NASA's Astronomy Picture of the Day, or APOD, shares a space image or video with an explanation. VERTEX shows APOD with a local backup if NASA data is unavailable.

exoplanets_and_life.name: Exoplanet
exoplanets_and_life.answer: Exoplanets are planets that orbit stars outside our solar system. Scientists have discovered thousands of them.

exoplanets_and_life.name: Habitable Zone
exoplanets_and_life.answer: The habitable zone is the region around a star where temperatures could allow liquid water on a planet's surface. It does not automatically mean life exists there.

exoplanets_and_life.name: Transit Method
exoplanets_and_life.answer: The transit method finds exoplanets by watching a star dim slightly when a planet crosses in front of it from our view.

exoplanets_and_life.name: Radial Velocity
exoplanets_and_life.answer: The radial velocity method finds planets by measuring tiny star wobbles caused by an orbiting planet's gravity.

exoplanets_and_life.name: Astrobiology
exoplanets_and_life.answer: Astrobiology is the study of possible life beyond Earth. Scientists look for water, useful chemistry, energy sources, and stable environments.

exoplanets_and_life.name: Biosignature
exoplanets_and_life.answer: A biosignature is a possible sign of life, such as certain gases in a planet's atmosphere. Scientists must be careful because non-living processes can sometimes make similar signs.
